title: Whakairo Awa: Carving the River's Story / Te Whakairo i te Kōrero o te Awa
subject: Arts
culturalContext: Students will explore the art of whakairo (carving) through the story of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (The Mangakōtukutuku Stream), a significant waterway that flows through our rohe. The awa holds deep cultural significance for Ngāti Kahungunu, serving as both a physical and spiritual landmark. According to local pūrākau, the stream was created by the tears of Ranginui (sky father) when separated from Papatūānuku (earth mother). Students will learn how traditional carving patterns (kowhaiwhai, tukutuku) tell the story of this awa and its connection to our people.
learningObjectives: Create a whakairo panel that visually interprets at least two elements from the pūrākau of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku using traditional Māori patterns
Analyze and explain how three different traditional carving patterns (e.g., puhoro, rauponga) represent natural elements of the local environment
Collaborate with peers to develop a collective artwork that demonstrates understanding of kaitiakitanga principles applied to the Mangakōtukutuku stream
Present artwork to the class using appropriate te reo Māori terms for carving techniques and patterns

activities.title: Whakarongo ki te Pūrākau: Listening to the River's Story
activities.description: Students will visit the banks of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with local kaumātua Hone Smith, who will share the creation pūrākau of the stream. Students will sketch natural elements and patterns they observe in the river's flow, surrounding vegetation, and rock formations, connecting these observations to potential carving patterns.

activities.title: Whakamātau i ngā Tauira: Pattern Exploration Workshop
activities.description: Working with local carver Rangi Thompson from Ngāti Kahungunu Whakairo Collective, students will practice fundamental carving techniques on soapstone blocks. They will learn to recreate traditional patterns like puhoro (representing swift movement, like river currents) and rauponga (representing the flax leaves along the riverbanks).

activities.title: Hono i te Ao Tawhito ki te Ao Hou: Traditional to Contemporary Translation
activities.description: Students will design their own contemporary carving pattern inspired by both traditional Māori designs and their personal observations of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. They must incorporate at least one element that addresses modern environmental challenges facing the stream (e.g., pollution, water quality).

activities.title: Waihanga Whakairo: Carving Creation
activities.description: Using locally sourced wood from sustainable plantations (primarily tōtara and mataī), students will create individual whakairo panels that tell a visual story of the Mangakōtukutuku stream, incorporating both traditional patterns and their original designs developed in previous activities.

activities.title: Whakaaturanga o ngā Mahi: Exhibition and Reflection
activities.description: Students will curate a class exhibition of their whakairo works at the college marae, presenting their pieces to whānau and community members. Each student will explain how their artwork connects to both the traditional pūrākau and contemporary kaitiakitanga of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku.

resources: Local kaumātua Hone Smith (expert in Ngāti Kahungunu pūrākau)
Carver Rangi Thompson from Ngāti Kahungunu Whakairo Collective
Digital archive: Te Ara Encyclopedia of New Zealand - Māori carving patterns
Sustainable wood supplies from Tāne Mahuta Native Plant Nursery (local provider)

tasks: Completed individual whakairo panel demonstrating technical skill and cultural understanding
Written reflection (500 words) explaining the cultural and environmental significance of the design choices
Oral presentation using te reo Māori terms for carving techniques and patterns
Peer assessment of collaborative elements in the final exhibition

nzcAlignment: Understanding the Arts in Context: Level 5 - Investigate the purpose of objects and images from past and present cultures
Developing Practical Knowledge: Level 5 - Apply knowledge of elements and principles for a specific purpose
Developing Ideas: Level 5 - Develop and refine ideas in selected art works in response to a variety of motivations
Communicating and Interpreting: Level 5 - Describe and evaluate how art works communicate ideas
